text: The surgeon incises the peritoneum at the sacral promontory below the aortic bifurcation. A medial to lateral dissection of the sigmoid and descending colon is performed along the avascular plane, preserving autonomic nerves. Under white light, the anatomical blood supply around the pancreas and inferior mesenteric vein is assessed. Five milligrams of ICG dye is injected, and near-infrared fluorescence imaging reveals the collateral artery near the inferior mesenteric vein and pancreas. The fluorescence highlights the low border of the pancreas, followed by the collateral artery and inferior mesenteric vein, appearing in sequence within five seconds of the ICG injection.

The surgeon lifts the inferior mesenteric vein using a double fenestrated grasper on the third robotic arm, incising the peritoneum beneath it to develop the retroperitoneal space. Under near-infrared fluorescence imaging, the optimal point for inferior mesenteric vein ligation is chosen and performed under white light with careful skeletonization. Lymph nodes around the root of the inferior mesenteric artery are dissected, followed by ICG injection to identify the left colic artery. The surgeon selects the division point under fluorescence imaging, and low ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery is performed, preserving the left colic branch. Blood flow in the preserved left colic artery is confirmed using near-infrared fluorescence imaging.
